key_points: Markets are part of a larger economic system connecting production, distribution, and consumption
Goods often move through multiple intermediaries including wholesalers, distributors, and retailers before reaching consumers
Operational costs such as rent, electricity, transport, and storage influence the final price of goods
Different markets cater to different social and economic groups based on affordability and accessibility
Competition among sellers influences prices, product quality, and consumer choices
examples: Vegetables grown by farmers transported to wholesale markets and then sold to retail vendors
Supermarkets and malls offering branded goods at fixed prices due to higher operational costs
Mobile traders participating in different weekly markets across towns
Small neighbourhood stores providing credit facilities to regular customers
Street vendors selling affordable products to low-income consumers
formulas: Market System → Network of producers, traders, and consumers
Supply Chain → Production → Wholesale → Retail → Consumption
Retail Price = Cost Price + Transport Cost + Storage Cost + Seller Margin
Consumer Choice → Influenced by price, availability, and convenience
exam_tips: Understand how the chain of markets works from producer to consumer
Focus on the economic factors that influence market prices
Learn how different markets serve different groups of consumers
Be able to explain the role of intermediaries in distribution of goods
